skill.name: tmux-processes
skill.description: Guidance for starting, monitoring, restarting, and stopping long-running development processes inside tmux sessions.
skill.summary: Run persistent development servers and watchers in project-scoped tmux sessions.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell and command-substitution examples because the skill documents tmux workflows. Review found those commands are central to the stated purpose and no malicious exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden network behavior was found. The weak cryptography alert at SKILL.md:3 is a false positive, but the skill still carries medium risk because it directs agents to start, monitor, and stop long-lived shell processes.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Shell Process Control
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create tmux sessions, send shell commands, capture process output, and restart or stop windows. This is legitimate for development workflows, but it can leave services running or interrupt project processes if used without checking the session target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and intentionally control tmux-managed shell processes. Confidence is high for operational risk, but there is no evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Device File References Are Benign
low_findings.description: The filesystem matches are limited to deriving a session name, redirecting stderr to /dev/null, and tailing an example log file. These are normal shell patterns and do not show secret collection or destructive file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced paths are standard shell redirection and an illustrative project log path. I found no evidence of broad file traversal, credential reads, or data staging.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The high-severity static alert points to the description front matter, which only describes long-lived tmux processes. No cryptographic function, hashing algorithm, encoded payload, or related implementation appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains plain descriptive metadata, not code. The static finding does not match the semantic content of the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains executable shell examples that invoke tmux, git, npm, rg, tail, grep, and sleep. These commands are expected for the skill, but users should review target sessions and commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly visible executable shell snippets. They support the advertised purpose and do not contain hidden network or obfuscated behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution for Session Naming
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly uses command substitution to derive the tmux session name from the git root or current directory. This is a normal automation pattern, but generated names should still be checked before kill operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution pattern is repeated and observable. Risk is operational rather than malicious because inputs come from the local project path and git metadata.

content.value_statement: Long-running dev servers and watchers can block an AI coding session or become hard to monitor. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code repeatable tmux patterns for starting, checking, and stopping project-scoped processes.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to create project-scoped tmux sessions for long-running commands.
Provides patterns for starting one process or multiple windowed processes.
Explains how to capture tmux output and search for errors or readiness messages.
Documents restart and shutdown commands for sessions and individual windows.
Includes idempotent checks to avoid starting duplicate dev server windows.
limitations: It is a Markdown guide and does not provide executable helper scripts.
It assumes tmux is installed and available in the shell environment.
It does not validate whether a project command is safe to run.
It does not manage processes outside tmux sessions.

use_cases.title: Start a Web App Dev Server
use_cases.description: Run a development server in tmux so the assistant can continue working while the server stays active.

use_cases.title: Monitor Watchers During Refactors
use_cases.description: Keep test and build watchers in named tmux windows and inspect recent output for failures.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Service Local Work
use_cases.description: Use one project session with separate windows for server, tests, and logs.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Start my project dev server in tmux using the project-scoped session pattern, then tell me the session and window name.

prompt_templates.title: Check Existing Process
prompt_templates.scenario: Inspect status before starting another process.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether a tmux session already exists for this project. If the server window exists, capture the last output instead of starting a duplicate.

prompt_templates.title: Run Server and Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Use multiple windows in one project session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create one tmux session for this project with separate windows for the dev server and test watcher. Use safe idempotent checks.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Readiness and Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the tmux server window for readiness and error messages. If the server is stale, restart only that window and confirm readiness.

output_examples.input: Start the app server in tmux.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a project-named tmux session, opens a server window, starts the dev command, and reports how to inspect recent output.

output_examples.input: Check whether the test watcher is still running.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists windows in the project session, checks the watcher window, and summarizes the latest captured status lines.
The assistant verifies the session name, sends an interrupt to the server window, restarts the command, and waits for a ready message.

best_practices: Use one tmux session per project and separate windows for related processes.
Capture recent output before deciding that a process needs a restart.
Verify the session and window name before stopping or killing anything.
anti_patterns: Do not use a global tmux kill command for project cleanup.
Do not start duplicate servers when a session or window already exists.
Do not use tmux for short one-shot commands that need direct output.

faq.question: What does this skill help manage?
faq.answer: It helps manage long-running terminal processes such as dev servers, test watchers, log tails, and Tilt sessions.

faq.question: Does it install tmux?
faq.answer: No. It assumes tmux is already installed and available in the current shell.

faq.question: Can it run multiple processes?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends one project session with separate named windows for each process.

faq.question: How does it reduce process conflicts?
faq.answer: It derives the session name from the project and checks for existing sessions or windows before starting new ones.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared machines?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill includes isolation rules, but users should verify names before stopping any process.

faq.question: Does it include executable scripts?
faq.answer: No. It is an instruction guide with command patterns that an assistant can apply.
